subject: ☁️ OpenAI Lands on AWS as Microsoft Exclusivity Ends
body_html:
Also: Google Moves Gemini to a Credit System  ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌

April 29, 2026   |   Read online

☁️ OpenAI Lands on AWS as Microsoft Exclusivity Ends

Also: Google Moves Gemini to a Credit System

 
 
 
 

In partnership with

 

 Subscribe | Advertise

Welcome, AI enthusiasts

Just a day after OpenAI loosened its ties with Microsoft, its most advanced models have landed on Amazon Bedrock, opening the door for broader enterprise access. Let’s dive in!

 

In today’s insights:

  • OpenAI Lands on AWS as Microsoft Exclusivity Ends

  • Google Moves Gemini to a Credit System

  • AI Spots Heart Disease Before It Strikes

Read time: 4 minutes

LATEST DEVELOPMENTS

OPENAI & AMAZON
☁️ OpenAI Lands on AWS as Microsoft Exclusivity Ends

Evolving AI: OpenAI's frontier models are now live on Amazon Bedrock, just one day after OpenAI and Microsoft tore up their exclusive partnership.

Key Points:

  • Microsoft's exclusive grip on OpenAI's models and APIs ended this week, replaced by a non-exclusive license running through 2032.

  • GPT-5.5, GPT-5.4, and the Codex coding agent are now in limited preview on Amazon Bedrock, with general availability in the coming weeks.

  • AWS is also launching Bedrock Managed Agents powered by OpenAI, giving enterprises a managed path to deploy production-grade AI agents.

Details:

On Monday, Microsoft and OpenAI rewrote their partnership, dropping the AGI-triggered exclusivity clause and clearing OpenAI to serve its products on any cloud. On Tuesday, AWS made the payoff public. Enterprises can now access OpenAI's frontier models, the Codex coding agent used by over 4 million people weekly, and managed agents through the same Bedrock APIs they already use. The two announcements are tightly linked: the shift unlocks the up to $50 billion Amazon investment in OpenAI announced in February, which Microsoft had publicly refuted and reportedly considered fighting in court.

Why It Matters:

For the first time, the world's most-used frontier models are decoupled from a single cloud. Enterprises gain real choice over where they run OpenAI workloads, and AWS gets a credible answer to Anthropic-on-Azure and Gemini-on-Google. The era of one lab tied to one hyperscaler is closing fast. When compute demand outgrows any single provider, can exclusive AI alliances survive at all?

TOGETHER WITH DATADOG
🔐 Monitoring Modern Infrastructure

Evolving AI: Your Guide to Monitoring Modern Infrastructure

Build resilient systems, reduce downtime, and gain full-stack visibility at scale. This in-depth eBook shows you how to evolve your monitoring strategy for today’s dynamic, cloud-native environments with:

  • Key principles of effective observability in modern architectures

  • Techniques for tracking performance across ephemeral infrastructure

  • Real-world examples of metrics, logs, and traces working together

Free Download →

GOOGLE
💰 Google Moves Gemini to a Credit System

Source: Google

Evolving AI: Google is preparing to replace Gemini's flat usage caps with a credit-based system, signaling a shift in how consumers pay for AI.

Key Points:

  • New strings in the Gemini app point to a monthly credit allowance with optional top-ups, replacing fixed prompt quotas.

  • A dedicated images section labeled "NEW" has appeared in the web UI, hinting at a built-in editor or upgraded model.

  • The shift aligns Google with OpenAI, Anthropic, and Notion, all of which already run on flexible consumption pricing.

Details:

Google is testing a model where users get a monthly pool of credits to spend across Gemini's models and features, instead of hitting hard prompt limits tied to their tier. The approach is already used in Flow, Whisk, and Antigravity, and extending it to the main app would unify billing across Google's AI stack. It also gives Google room to introduce premium features without forcing the steep jump from AI Pro at $19.99 to AI Ultra at $249.99. A likely unveil window is Google I/O on May 19 and 20.

Why It Matters:

Flat subscriptions are quietly disappearing from frontier AI. As models grow more agentic and compute costs climb, providers are pushing usage risk back onto users. That makes AI more flexible for power users but harder to budget for casual ones. The real question is whether consumers will accept paying by the task, the way developers already do.

AI is Changing How Athletes Train. Investors Are Already In.

AI is rewriting athletic performance.

Sparrow applies AI to human movement, giving athletes and coaches real-time feedback to train, recover, and perform better.

It’s already caught the attention of PGA and LPGA winners and 12 angel investor groups.

So far there's been 85% revenue growth, 250K users, and it's a $1 Trillion+ market.

Invest by 5/31 and receive 10% bonus shares. The round is nearly full.

Get in early: Invest in Sparrow now.

This is a paid advertisement for Sparrow's Regulation CF offering. Please read the offering circular at invest.sparrowup.com.

STEVE BEUERLEIN
 ❤️ AI Spots Heart Disease Before It Strikes

Source: Gilbert Flores / Variety / Getty Images

Evolving AI: Super Bowl champ Steve Beuerlein is using AI-powered heart imaging to detect cardiovascular risk years before symptoms appear.

Key Points:

  • Beuerlein, 61, enrolled in Heartflow's Gamefilm registry to screen for heart disease, motivated by his father's 30-year battle with the condition.

  • The AI builds a rotatable 3D model of the heart, color-coding zones where hard and soft plaque are starting to form.

  • Around 50% of first-time heart attacks happen with no prior warning signs, which is the gap this kind of early detection is designed to close.

Details:

Heartflow's platform uses AI to turn a non-invasive scan into a detailed 3D map of the patient's coronary arteries. Doctors and patients then review the results together on screen, rotating the heart and inspecting each chamber. The system flags hard plaque as an immediate concern and tracks softer buildup that often goes unnoticed. The goal is to give people a clear baseline so they can act on heart risk early, rather than waiting for a first cardiac event to reveal the problem.

Why It Matters:

Cardiovascular disease remains the world's leading killer, and most first-time events arrive with no warning. AI-driven imaging shifts heart care from reactive to preventive, turning a quiet, invisible risk into something patients can actually see and address. As these tools spread beyond specialty clinics, the bigger question becomes who gets access to predictive screening, and who is left waiting for symptoms?
